822	\subsection{The affine base-change lemma and its remaining obligations}
823	
824	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
825	reduces, via the locality criterion
826	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
827	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
828	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from
829	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
830	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
831	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
832	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
833	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
834	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
835	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
836	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
837	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
838	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
839	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
840	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
841	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.
842	
843	\begin{lemma}
844	
845	\leanok
846	[Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map]
847	  \label{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}
848	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_map_affine_local}
849	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
850	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
851	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
852	  % "local on $S$ and $S'$" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
853	  % L920--926).
854	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
855	  % assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
856	  % $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
857	  % for some $A$-module $M$."
858	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
859	  change'' (proof, local-on-\(S\)-and-\(S'\) step).}
860	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
861	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, with a base-change square as above. The formation of the
862	  base-change map (Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}) is compatible
863	  with restriction to affine opens of \(S'\): for each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\), the
864	  restriction of the base-change map to sections over \(U\) agrees with the
865	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
866	  by the affine-open locality criterion
867	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
868	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
869	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
870	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
871	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
872	
873	\end{lemma}
874	
875	\begin{proof}
876	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
877	  By the affine-open locality criterion
878	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the base-change map is an isomorphism
879	  of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules as soon as its restriction to the sections over each
880	  affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
881	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
882	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
883	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
884	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
885	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
886	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
887	
888	  \medskip
889	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
890	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, the base-change map is the image,
891	  under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
892	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
893	  \[
894	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
895	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
896	  \]
897	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
898	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
899	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
900	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
901	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
902	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
903	
904	  \medskip
905	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
906	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
907	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
908	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
909	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
910	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
911	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
912	  \begin{itemize}
913	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
914	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
915	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
916	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
917	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
918	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
919	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
920	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
921	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
922	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
923	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
924	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
925	  \end{itemize}
926	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
927	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
928	  restricted counterpart — shows that the restriction of the base-change map to
929	  sections over \(U\) is itself the base-change map formed from the cartesian square
930	  restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
931	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
932	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
933	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
934	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
935	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
936	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
937	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
938	  obligation beyond them.
939	
940	  \medskip
941	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
942	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
943	  that the base-change map restricts to an isomorphism on sections over each affine
944	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2, precisely the
945	  affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with \emph{no} flatness
946	  hypothesis, by the section-level computation
947	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
948	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
949	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion
950	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} to this family of per-open
Let me continue reading the blueprint and also start reading the Lean file.
Let me continue reading the blueprint and start reading the Lean file in parallel.

951	  isomorphisms shows that the base-change map is an isomorphism, as asserted.
952	\end{proof}

954	\subsection{The section-level base-change identity}
955	
956	The section-level identification
957	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} below is the single
958	highest-leverage obligation of the affine close. In the affine--affine model the
959	base-change map \(\theta\) is, after its domain and codomain are read through the two
960	proved tilde dictionaries, a concrete \(R'\)-linear map
961	\(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and the content of the lemma is
962	that this map is the inverse of the cancellation isomorphism
963	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Because the base-change map is \emph{defined} as the
964	transpose of the inner pushforward comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) under the
965	\((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunction (\(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\)), its section-level value
966	is read off by the \emph{conjugate-counit calculus}: unfolding the transpose through the
967	counit form of the hom-set adjunction
968	(\(\operatorname{Adjunction.homEquiv\_counit}\)) factors \(\theta\) as
969	\(g^*(\theta_{\mathrm{in}})\) followed by the geometric counit \(\varepsilon_g\), and
970	conjugating this composite by the two tilde dictionaries
971	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and
972	\(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) replaces the
973	geometric counit by the \emph{algebraic} extension-of-scalars counit along \(\psi\). This
974	is the route the live formalization takes: the cancellation identity
975	Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} routes through the generator
976	trace Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_generator_trace}, which is a one-line corollary of the
977	section identity Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_section_identity}, whose single residual
978	obligation is the counit-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_transpose}.
979	The per-generator/element evaluation is \emph{not} available as a shortcut: evaluating the
980	opaque geometric composite on \(1 \otimes m\) produces no element-level normal form that
981	\(\operatorname{rfl}\) or \(\operatorname{simp}\) can reduce, so the abstract conjugate
982	calculus is the only tractable discharge. Two Mathlib results are recorded
983	first as dependency anchors.
984

1244	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1245	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1246	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1247	% used in the codomain-read computation.

1249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1250	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1251	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1253	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1254	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1255	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1256	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1257	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1258	\end{lemma}
1259	\begin{proof}
1260	
1261	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1262	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1263	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1264	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1265	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1266	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1267	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1268	\end{proof}
1269	
1270	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1271	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1272	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1274	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1275	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1276	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1277	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1278	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1279	\end{lemma}
1280	\begin{proof}
1281	
1282	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1284	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1285	\end{proof}

1291	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1292	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1293	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1295	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1296	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1297	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1298	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1299	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1300	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1301	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1302	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1303	  % L933--938).
1304	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1305	  % equality
1306	  % $$
1307	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1308	  % $$
1309	  % as $R'$-modules."
1310	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1311	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1312	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1313	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1314	  \[
1315	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1316	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1317	  \]
1318	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1319	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1320	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1321	  hypothesis.
1322	\end{lemma}
1323	
1324	\begin{proof}
1325	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1326	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1327	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1328	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1329	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1330	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1331	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1332	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1333	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1334	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1335	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1336	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1337	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1338	
1339	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1340	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1341	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1342	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1343	  dictionaries,
1344	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1345	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1346	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1347	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1348	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1349	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1350	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1351	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1352	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1353	  \[
1354	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1355	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1356	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1357	  \]
1358	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1359	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1360	
1361	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1362	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1363	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1364	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1365	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1366	\end{proof}

1490	    \;=\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{f,g})^{\mathrm{hom}}.

1499	\begin{lemma}[Conjugation is an equivalence on isomorphisms]
1500	  \label{lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib}
1501	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateIsoEquiv}
1502	  \mathlibok
1503	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1504	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\) be two adjunctions
1505	  between the same pair of categories. The conjugation (mate) bijection on natural transformations
1506	  restricts to an equivalence on natural \emph{isomorphisms},
1507	  \[
1508	    \operatorname{conjIso}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2) :
1509	      (L_2 \cong L_1) \;\simeq\; (R_1 \cong R_2),
1510	  \]
1511	  carrying an isomorphism of left adjoints to the conjugate isomorphism of right adjoints, and back. In
1512	  particular the conjugate of an isomorphism is an isomorphism: this is the certificate that makes the
1513	  affine flat-base-change comparison invertible without inspecting its concrete sheaf assembly.
1514	\end{lemma}
1515	
1516	\begin{lemma}[The iterated mate of a left-adjoint square is the conjugate (Beck--Chevalley)]
1517	  \label{lem:iterated_mateEquiv_conjugateEquiv_mathlib}
1518	  \lean{CategoryTheory.iterated_mateEquiv_conjugateEquiv}
1519	  \mathlibok
1520	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1521	  For a square of functors whose four boundary functors are all left adjoints, with adjunctions
1522	  \(\mathrm{adj}_1, \dots, \mathrm{adj}_4\), the iterated mate (the Beck--Chevalley \(2\)-cell of the
1523	  pasted square) of a \(2\)-cell \(\alpha\) equals the conjugate of \(\alpha\) for the two composite
1524	  adjunctions:
1525	  \[
1526	    \operatorname{mate}\bigl(\operatorname{mate}(\alpha)\bigr) \;=\;
1527	      \operatorname{conj}\bigl(\mathrm{adj}_1.\mathrm{adj}_4,\ \mathrm{adj}_3.\mathrm{adj}_2\bigr)\,\alpha .
1528	  \]
1529	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateIsoEquiv_mathlib} this is the abstract reason a Beck--Chevalley
1530	  transformation is a natural isomorphism exactly when \(\alpha\) is — the conceptual home of the
1531	  iso-ness of the affine comparison \(g^* f_*\widetilde M \to f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M\), which the
1532	  project realises directly as a conjugate of the pushforward \(\Gamma\)-comparison.
1533	\end{lemma}

1535	\begin{lemma}[Composite-adjunction comparison from a right-adjoint isomorphism]
1536	  \label{lem:leftAdjointCompIso_mathlib}

1538	  \mathlibok
1539	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1540	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_{01} : F_{01} \dashv G_{10}\), \(\mathrm{adj}_{12} : F_{12} \dashv G_{21}\) and
1541	  \(\mathrm{adj}_{02} : F_{02} \dashv G_{20}\) be adjunctions, and let
1542	  \(e : G_{21} \circ G_{10} \cong G_{20}\) be a natural isomorphism comparing the composite right adjoint
1543	  with \(G_{20}\). Then there is a canonical natural isomorphism of the corresponding left adjoints
1544	  \[
1545	    \operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}(e) : F_{01} \circ F_{12} \;\cong\; F_{02},
1546	  \]
1547	  namely the conjugate of \(e^{-1}\). It builds the comparison for a composite of two adjunctions out of
1548	  a single right-adjoint comparison, carrying no propositional equality data.

1551	\begin{lemma}[The conjugate of the composite-comparison inverse is the right-adjoint isomorphism]
1552	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_leftAdjointCompIso_inv_mathlib}

1557	  composite adjunction \(\mathrm{adj}_{01}.\mathrm{adj}_{12}\) and \(\mathrm{adj}_{02}\) sends the inverse
1558	  of the composite comparison \(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}(e)\) back to the hom of the

1562	      \bigl(\operatorname{leftAdjointCompIso}(e)^{-1}\bigr) \;=\; e^{\mathrm{hom}} .

1565	  equation on the right-adjoint side, where the conjugate simp set closes it.

1663	\begin{lemma}
1664	
1665	\leanok
1666	[Pseudofunctoriality of the pullback--pushforward unit]
1667	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1669	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1670	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1671	  Let \(a : X_1 \to X_2\) and \(b : X_2 \to X_3\) be composable morphisms of schemes and let
1672	  \(N\) be an \(\mathcal{O}_{X_3}\)-module. Write \(\eta^{h}\) for the unit of the
1673	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1674	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1675	  composition coherences. Then
1676	  \[
1677	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1678	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1679	    \;=\;
1680	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1681	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1682	  \]
1683	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1684	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1685	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1686	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1687	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg
1688	  unit into the affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-unit modulo the transparent coherences.
1689	\end{lemma}
1690	\begin{proof}

1692	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1693	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1694	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1695	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1696	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1697	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by
1698	  Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib} that mate is exactly the pushforward
1699	  coherence \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}\). Expanding the unit of the composite
1700	  adjunction by Lemma~\ref{lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib} —

1702	    = \eta^{b}_N \circ b_{*}(\eta^{a}_{b^{*}N})\) — and reassociating the resulting composite gives
1703	  the stated equation.

9	/-!
10	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
11	
12	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
13	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
14	base change on the target.
15	
16	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
17	```
18	  X' --g'--> X
19	  |f'        |f
20	  v          v
21	  S' --g---> S
22	```
23	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
24	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
25	
26	The three main declarations are:
27	
28	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
29	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
30	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
31	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
32	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
33	  associativity).
34	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
35	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
36	
37	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
38	
39	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
40	Tag 02KH.
41	-/
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits
46	
47	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
48	
49	open Scheme.Modules
50	
51	open scoped TensorProduct
52	
53	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
54	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
55	
56	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
57	
58	Given a commutative square
59	```
60	  X' --g'--> X
61	  |f'        |f
62	  v          v
63	  S' --g---> S
64	```
65	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
66	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
67	
68	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
69	```
70	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
71	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
72	```
73	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
74	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
75	commutativity of the square.
76	
77	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
78	base-change diagram. -/
79	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
80	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
81	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
82	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
83	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
84	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
85	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
86	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)
87

1937	\begin{lemma}[Conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
1938	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
1939	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp}
1940	  \mathlibok
1941	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1942	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\), \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\),
1943	  \(\mathrm{adj}_3 : L_3 \dashv R_3\) be adjunctions between the same pair of categories, and let
1944	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\), \(\beta : L_3 \Rightarrow L_2\) be transformations of left adjoints.
1945	  Then the conjugates compose, with order reversed onto the right-adjoint side:
1946	  \[
1947	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \;\circ\;
1948	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
1949	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha) .
1950	  \]
1951	  Tagged \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\), it is the lead member of the conjugate
1952	  reassociation simp set: it fuses the per-adjunction conjugates of a composite adjunction into the
1953	  conjugate of the composite (the conjugate-side form of the paste-of-mates).
1954	\end{lemma}
1955	
1956	\begin{lemma}[Inverse conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
1957	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_symm_comp_mathlib}
1958	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_symm_comp}
1959	  \mathlibok
1960	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1961	  The inverse-direction companion of Lemma~\ref{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}: for transformations
1962	  \(\alpha : R_1 \Rightarrow R_2\), \(\beta : R_2 \Rightarrow R_3\) of the right adjoints,
1963	  \[
1964	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)^{-1}\beta \;\circ\;
1965	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)^{-1}\alpha
1966	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)^{-1}(\alpha \circ \beta) ,
1967	  \]
1968	  also \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\). Together these two close the reassociation bookkeeping of
1969	  the conjugate-side discharge.
1970	\end{lemma}

2568	  \uses{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
2569	  Let \(e : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes and \(N\) a \(Y\)-module. Then the unit
2570	  \(\eta^{e}_{N} : N \to e_{*}\,e^{*}N\) of the \((e^{*} \dashv e_{*})\)-adjunction is an
2571	  isomorphism. This is the single fact enabling cancellation~(1): in the inner telescoping it is
2572	  the \(e\)-unit factor \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\eta^{e}\) (here \(e\) is the canonical
2573	  square isomorphism \(\mathrm{pullbackSpecIso}\)), which the codomain read
2574	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) bakes in as its inverse \(\eta^{e}\)-piece, so the two cancel.

2579	  is an equivalence (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}). The unit of an adjunction whose
2580	  left adjoint is an equivalence is an isomorphism; hence \(\eta^{e}_{N}\) is invertible.
2581	\end{proof}

2773	  \(\eta^{\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}_{\widetilde M}\), whose section value over
2774	  \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) is the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of
2775	  Seam~1 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). This is the residual telescoping that closes
2776	  the leg-parametrised reindex; it is the single remaining target of Seam~A.
2777	\end{lemma}
2778	\begin{proof}
2779	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit,
2780	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp,
2781	        lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp,
2782	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs,
2783	        lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id,
2784	        lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}
2785	  The four distributed \(\Gamma\)-image factors are cancelled against the unfolded leg-parametrised
2786	  codomain read Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read_legs} by the three one-cancellation
2787	  atoms. Carrying the legs as free parameters and identifying them with
2788	  \(e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\) definitionally removes the dependent-motive obstruction of
2789	  the literal-leg form.
2790	
2791	  \emph{The residual object-form diamond.} After distributing \(\Gamma\) over the composite, the
2792	  factor to be collapsed sits over the \emph{nested-image} object \(G(H(M))\) — the form produced
2793	  by distributing a functor over a composite of morphisms — whereas the collapsing atom fixes the
2794	  same object in its \emph{composed-functor} form \((H \circ G)(M)\), the form in which the
2795	  pushforward-composition coherence isomorphism presents its (co)domain. These two object
2796	  presentations are definitionally but not syntactically equal, creating a diamond that recurs at
2797	  each of the three cancellations.
2798	
2799	  \emph{The resolution: congruence lifting.} Each factor-equation is lifted into the ambient
2800	  composite \emph{one neighbour at a time}: a factor identity \(u = u'\) is promoted to
2801	  \(c \circ u = c \circ u'\), or to \(u \circ c = u' \circ c\), by congruence in the composition
2802	  operation with the neighbouring factor \(c\) held fixed, and the promoted steps are chained in
2803	  sequence. The chain is then closed on the definitional bridge between the nested-image and
2804	  composed-functor presentations of the shared object, absorbing the object-form diamond at that
2805	  single closing step. The three atoms
2806	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit},
2807	  \ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp},
2808	  \ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp}) are supplied in the
2809	  \(\Gamma \circ (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_{*}\) form and are spliced directly into the composite
2810	  by this congruence chaining.
2811	
2812	  \emph{The three cancellations.} Cancellation~(1): the \(e\)-unit is invertible
2813	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_eUnit}) and meets its inverse inside the codomain
2814	  read. Cancellation~(2): the surviving pushforward-composition coherence has identity
2815	  \(\Gamma\)-image (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pushforwardComp}, whose
2816	  section-level content is the collapse Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id}).
2817	  Cancellation~(3): the trailing pullback-comparison factor cancels the inverse the codomain read
2818	  bakes in (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_inner_eCancel_pullbackComp}). The lone survivor is the
2819	  affine \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit, evaluated by Seam~1
2820	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}) to \(\rho\).
2821	\end{proof}

103	    (φ : M ⟶ N) :

105	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by

107	  · intro h x
108	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := Functor.map_isIso _ _
109	    exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
110	  · intro h
111	    -- Package the underlying abelian presheaves as `TopCat.Sheaf`es.
112	    let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
113	    let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
114	    let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩

117	    have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) :=
118	      (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS

122	opens of `X` and `φ : M ⟶ N` restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every basic
123	open `B i`, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This reduces iso-checking from *all* opens (the
124	content of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`) to a chosen basis. Project-local:
125	Mathlib provides the stalkwise pieces (`germ_exist_of_isBasis`,
126	`stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis`) but not the packaged criterion at the
127	`Scheme.Modules` level. -/
128	theorem Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
129	    {ι : Type*} {B : ι → X.Opens} (hB : TopologicalSpace.Opens.IsBasis (Set.range B))
130	    (φ : M ⟶ N) (h : ∀ i, IsIso (φ.app (B i))) : IsIso φ := by
131	  -- Reduce to a stalkwise isomorphism of the underlying `Ab`-presheaf morphism `α`.
132	  rw [Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map]
133	  intro x
134	  -- `α.app (op (B i))` is definitionally `φ.app (B i)`, hence an isomorphism on each basic open.
135	  have happ : ∀ U ∈ Set.range B,
136	      IsIso (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U)) := by
137	    rintro U ⟨i, rfl⟩; exact h i
138	  rw [CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
139	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
140	  · -- Injectivity of the stalk map from injectivity on a basis.
141	    refine TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis hB ?_ x
142	    intro U hU
143	    haveI := happ U hU
144	    exact (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
145	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).injective
146	  · -- Surjectivity: a germ at `x` comes from a section over a basic open, where `α` is onto.
147	    intro t
148	    obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU, s, rfl⟩ :=
149	      TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist_of_isBasis hB N.presheaf x t
150	    haveI := happ U hU
151	    obtain ⟨s', hs'⟩ := (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
152	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).surjective s
153	    refine ⟨M.presheaf.germ U x hxU s', ?_⟩
154	    erw [TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_germ_apply]
155	    rw [hs']
156	    rfl

268	theorem globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}} (φ : R ⟶ R') :

270	      = φ ≫ (StructureSheaf.globalSectionsIso ↑R').hom := by

277	
278	/-- **Global sections of an affine pushforward = restriction of scalars.** For a ring
279	map `φ : R ⟶ R'` and *any* sheaf of modules `N` on `Spec R'`, the `R`-module of global
280	sections of the pushforward `(Spec φ)_* N` is the restriction of scalars along `φ` of the
281	`R'`-module of global sections of `N`. Built element-free (route (b)): both sides peel
282	(by `rfl`) to nested `ModuleCat.restrictScalars` towers over the common global-section
283	module `Γ(N, ⊤)`; the two towers are reconciled by collapsing each via
284	`ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App` and applying the ring equation
285	`globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop`. No element-level scalar multiplication is
286	touched, dodging the carrier-instance wall. Project-local: the affine companion of the
287	global-sections fragment of flat base change. -/
288	noncomputable def gammaPushforwardIso {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}} (φ : R ⟶ R')
289	    (N : (Spec R').Modules) :
290	    (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R)).obj ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).obj N) ≅
291	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj ((moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).obj N) := by
292	  set SecN : ModuleCat ↑((Spec R').ringCatSheaf.obj.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) :=
293	    (((SheafOfModules.forgetToSheafModuleCat (Spec R').ringCatSheaf (Opposite.op ⊤)
294	        (Limits.initialOpOfTerminal Limits.isTerminalTop)).obj N).val.obj (Opposite.op ⊤)) with hSecN
295	  set gsRhom := (StructureSheaf.globalSectionsIso ↑R).hom.hom with hgsR
296	  set gsR'hom := (StructureSheaf.globalSectionsIso ↑R').hom.hom with hgsR'
297	  set pushTop := ((Spec.map φ).toRingCatSheafHom.hom.app (Opposite.op ⊤)).hom with hpush
298	  have hcomp : pushTop.comp gsRhom = gsR'hom.comp φ.hom := by
299	    apply RingHom.ext
300	    intro x
301	    simpa [hpush, hgsR, hgsR', RingHom.comp_apply] using
302	      congr($(globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop φ).hom x)
303	  exact (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App gsRhom pushTop (pushTop.comp gsRhom) rfl SecN).symm ≪≫
304	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsCongr hcomp).app SecN ≪≫
305	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalarsComp'App φ.hom gsR'hom (gsR'hom.comp φ.hom) rfl SecN)
306	
307	/-- **Γ-fragment for a tilde-module.** Specialising `gammaPushforwardIso` to `N = M^~`
308	and using the unit iso `tilde.toTildeΓNatIso` (`Γ(M^~, ⊤) ≅ M`), the `R`-module of global
309	sections of `(Spec φ)_* (M^~)` is the restriction of scalars of `M` along `φ`. This is the
310	`Γ`-fragment comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map in the affine
311	reduction of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`. Project-local: corollary of
312	`gammaPushforwardIso`. -/
313	noncomputable def gammaPushforwardTildeIso {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}} (φ : R ⟶ R')
314	    (M : ModuleCat.{u} R') :
315	    (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R)).obj
316	        ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M)) ≅
317	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M :=
318	  gammaPushforwardIso φ (tilde M) ≪≫
319	    (ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).mapIso (tilde.toTildeΓNatIso.app M).symm
320	
321	/-- **Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open = restriction of scalars.**
322	The `D(a)`-level (indeed arbitrary-open) generalization of `gammaPushforwardIso`. For a ring map
323	`φ : R ⟶ R'`, an `Spec R'`-module `N`, and an open `U` of `Spec R` with preimage
324	`V = (Spec φ)⁻¹ U` in `Spec R'`, the `R`-module of sections of the pushforward
325	`(Spec φ)_* N` over `U` is the restriction of scalars along `φ` of the `R'`-module of sections of
326	`N` over `V`. Because `modulesSpecToSheaf` forgets to the *global* section ring uniformly (it
327	restricts scalars along the global-sections map at the top open, not at `U`), the construction is
328	*identical* to that of `gammaPushforwardIso` — the same restriction-of-scalars composition identity
329	twice plus the same `⊤`-level ring equation `globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop` — with only
330	the evaluation open changed from `⊤` to `U` / `V`. Project-local. -/
331	noncomputable def gammaPushforwardIsoAt {R R' : CommRingCat.{u}} (φ : R ⟶ R')
332	    (N : (Spec R').Modules) (U : (Spec R).Opens) :
333	    (modulesSpecToSheaf.obj ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).obj N)).val.obj
334	        (Opposite.op U) ≅
335	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj

2892	[(B) Generator close: the base change of $\rho$ is the inverse regrouping]
2893	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2894	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}
2895	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2896	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma `lemma-affine-base-change'
2897	  % (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the "boils down to the equality" step
2898	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L933--938).
2899	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the equality
2900	  % $$ (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M $$ as $R'$-modules."
2901	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base change''
2902	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
2903	  Let \(\rho : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), be the
2904	  inner comparison value of Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}. The extension of
2905	  scalars along \(\psi : R \to R'\) of \(\rho\), post-composed with the algebraic counit
2906	  \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) of the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\),
2907	  is an \(R'\)-linear map \(R' \otimes_R M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\); on the generator
2908	  \(r' \otimes m\) it returns \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\),
2909	  which is exactly the value of the inverse regrouping isomorphism
2910	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on \(r' \otimes m\). Both maps being
2911	  \(R'\)-linear and agreeing on the generators \(r' \otimes m\), they coincide:
2912	  \[
2913	    \operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \,\circ\, \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}
2914	      \;=\; \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}.
2915	  \]
2916	\end{lemma}
2917	\begin{proof}
2918	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2919	  Both sides are \(R'\)-linear maps out of \(R' \otimes_R M\), so by extensionality it suffices to
2920	  check the value on the generators \(r' \otimes m\). The left side sends
2921	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \rho(m) = r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) =
2922	  (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), using that \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) reads the extension of
2923	  scalars by acting through the \(R'\)-scalar. The inverse regrouping isomorphism of
2924	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} sends, by definition,
2925	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\) (in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation). The two
2926	  values agree on every generator, so the maps are equal. No flatness hypothesis is used.
2927	\end{proof}

3090	    \Pi_{\psi}^{-1} \circ g^*\!\bigl(\varepsilon^{\Gamma}\bigr) \circ \varepsilon_g ,
3091	  \]
3092	  where \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) is the algebraic extension/restriction-of-scalars counit
3093	  along \(\psi\), \(\varepsilon^{\Gamma}\) the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit, and \(\Pi_{\psi}\) the
3094	  pullback dictionary (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}); the dictionary enters because the
3095	  conjugate of \(\beta^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is \(\Pi_{\psi}^{-1}\). The trailing dictionary and
3096	  tilde-counit factors are isomorphisms, so solving this identity for \(\varepsilon_g\) and applying
3097	  \(\Gamma_{R'}\) rewrites \(\Gamma_{R'}(\varepsilon_g)\) in the goal into the algebraic counit
3098	  conjugated by exactly the dictionaries that \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)
3099	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}) and \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
3100	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) bake in. The remaining \(\sim\)150 lines of
3101	  telescoping are three steps.
3102	
3103	  \emph{(a) Inner reindex} (\(\Gamma_R(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}) = \rho\)). The \(\operatorname{Spec} R\)
3104	  section reading of the inner comparison \(\theta_{\mathrm{in}}\) is the canonical \(R\)-linear map
3105	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) (the affine inner value). This is reproven
3106	  \emph{inline}, not cited from the leg-reindex wrapper: expand the buried \((g')\)-unit by the
3107	  leg-unit expansion Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_unitExpand} (whose engine is
3108	  the proved unit-distribution Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}), distribute the
3109	  resulting four factors through \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\) and \(\Gamma\) by
3110	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex_legs_gammaDistribute}, collapse the
3111	  pushforward-composition coherences to the identity \(\Gamma\)-image
3112	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_hom_eq_id},
3113	  \ref{lem:gammaMap_pushforwardComp_inv_eq_id}), and evaluate the lone surviving affine unit by
3114	  Seam~1 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}); the result is \(\rho\). With the inner value
3115	  available the residual \(g^*\)-leg, conjugated by \(\Pi_{\psi}\), is
3116	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho)\).
3117	
3118	  \emph{(b) One-generator close.} The composite
3119	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \circ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) is an \(R'\)-linear map
3120	  \(R' \otimes_R M \to (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\); by \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\)
3121	  extensionality it suffices to evaluate it on the generator \(r' \otimes m\), where it returns
3122	  \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\). This is exactly the value of the
3123	  inverse regrouping isomorphism \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\) on \(r' \otimes m\), so a
3124	  one-generator \(\operatorname{ext}\) against Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} gives
3125	  \(\operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \circ \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}} = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\).
3126	
3127	  \emph{(c) Dictionary cancellation.} It remains to match the \(\Pi_{\psi}\) (pullback dictionary,
3128	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}) and tilde-counit factors appearing in the master
3129	  transport identity against the \(\operatorname{pushforward}\)- and \(\operatorname{pullback}\)-tilde
3130	  dictionaries baked into the domain read \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\) and codomain read
3131	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). These factors are mutually inverse isomorphisms, and cancelling them
3132	  reduces the conjugated geometric transpose to the algebraic composite of step~(b), namely
3133	  \(\operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\). This is the claimed equation
3134	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \ggg \Gamma(\theta) \ggg \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}
3135	  = \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}\).
3136	\end{proof}

3292	\begin{lemma}
3293	[Direct section-level value of the affine base-change map]
3294	  \label{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_sections_direct}

3321	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
3322	% `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
3323	% "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
3324	% L927--938).
3325	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
3326	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
3327	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
3328	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
3329	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
3330	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
3331	% equality
3332	% $$
3333	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
3334	% $$
3335	% as $R'$-modules."
3336	\begin{proof}
3337	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map,
3338	        lem:base_change_mate_domain_read,
3339	        lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read,
3340	        lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso,
3341	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso,
3342	        lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
3343	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
3344	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
3345	
3346	  The proof is a direct module computation: we unfold the \emph{definition} of
3347	  \(\alpha = \mathtt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\) and evaluate each adjunction
3348	  unit/counit through the single-step tilde dictionaries, never invoking the
3349	  composite-mate recognition. By
3350	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \(\alpha\) is the
3351	  \((g^* \dashv g_*)\)-adjunct of the map
3352	  \[
3353	    \beta : f_*\widetilde M \longrightarrow g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M
3354	          = f_*\,(g')_*(g')^*\widetilde M
3355	  \]
3356	  obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
3357	  \(\eta : \widetilde M \to (g')_*(g')^*\widetilde M\) of the
3358	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the square's commutativity
3359	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Explicitly, as adjunct,
3360	  \(\alpha = \varepsilon_{f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M} \circ g^*(\beta)\), where
3361	  \(\varepsilon\) is the counit of \(g^* \dashv g_*\). We trace each factor on global
3362	  sections over the affine model, on a generator \(r' \otimes m \in R' \otimes_R M\).
3363	
3364	  \emph{Step 1 — the inner unit \(\eta\) is the extension-of-scalars unit.}
3365	  On \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), the projection \(g'\) is the
3366	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map of the algebra inclusion \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\)
3367	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}). By the pullback dictionary
3368	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso}, \((g')^*\widetilde M\) is the tilde of the
3369	  extension of scalars \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\), and that lemma identifies the
3370	  adjunction unit with the algebraic unit of extension of scalars; hence on sections
3371	  \(\eta\) is the \(A\)-linear map
3372	  \[
3373	    M \longrightarrow (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M, \qquad
3374	    m \longmapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m .
3375	  \]
3376	  This is forced by the universal property of the tensor product and the algebra map
3377	  \(A \to R' \otimes_R A\); no mate identity is used.
3378	
3379	  \emph{Step 2 — apply \(f_* = \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\).} The pushforward
3380	  dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} reads \(f_*\) of a tilde as
3381	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Restriction of scalars does not move
3382	  elements, so on sections over \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) the map
3383	  \(\beta = f_*(\eta)\) (with codomain rewritten by \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\)) is the
3384	  same assignment \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), now read \(R\)-linearly into
3385	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\).
3386	
3387	  \emph{Step 3 — apply \(g^* = R' \otimes_R (-)\).} The pullback dictionary
3388	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} for \(g = \operatorname{Spec}\psi\) reads
3389	  \(g^*\) of a tilde as extension of scalars \(R' \otimes_R (-)\) along \(\psi\), and is
3390	  functorial: \(g^*(\beta)\) on sections over \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) is
3391	  \(\operatorname{id}_{R'} \otimes \beta\),
3392	  \[
3393	    R' \otimes_R M \longrightarrow R' \otimes_R \bigl((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\bigr),
3394	    \qquad
3395	    r' \otimes m \longmapsto r' \otimes \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr).
3396	  \]
3397	  The domain \(R' \otimes_R M\) here is exactly \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}\)'s reading of
3398	  \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde M))\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_domain_read}).
3399	
3400	  \emph{Step 4 — the counit \(\varepsilon\) is the extension-of-scalars counit.}
3401	  Writing \(W = (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) for the target \(R'\)-module, the counit
3402	  \(\varepsilon : g^* g_* \widetilde W \to \widetilde W\) of \(g^* \dashv g_*\) is, on
3403	  sections over \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and again by
3404	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} (which identifies the counit with the
3405	  algebraic action map of extension of scalars), the \(R'\)-linear map
3406	  \[
3407	    R' \otimes_R \bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\psi W\bigr) \longrightarrow W,
3408	    \qquad r' \otimes w \longmapsto r' \cdot w ,
3409	  \]
3410	  where \(r' \cdot w\) is the \(R'\)-action on \(W\). The codomain \(W\) is exactly
3411	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)'s reading of \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde M)\)
3412	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}).
3413	
3414	  \emph{Composition.} Composing Steps 3 and 4, the conjugated section map sends
3415	  \[
3416	    r' \otimes m
3417	    \;\overset{g^*(\beta)}{\longmapsto}\;
3418	    r' \otimes \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
3419	    \;\overset{\varepsilon}{\longmapsto}\;
3420	    r' \cdot \bigl((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\bigr)
3421	    = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m ,
3422	  \]
3423	  the last equality because the \(R'\)-action on \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)
3424	  multiplies into the \(R'\) tensor slot: \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m)
3425	  = ((r' \cdot 1) \otimes 1) \otimes m = (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). Thus
3426	  \(\Theta_{\mathrm{src}}^{-1} \ggg \Gamma(\alpha) \ggg \Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\) is the
3427	  \(R'\)-linear map \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\), which is
3428	  precisely \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}^{-1}\) of
3429	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
3430	  (\(\mathtt{cancelBaseChange\_symm\_tmul}\)). No flatness hypothesis is used, and the
3431	  value falls out of the tensor-product universal property together with the algebra

3583	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module need not be of this form

3602	% $$

3605	% as $R'$-modules."
3606	% NOTE: This affine lemma is closed directly on global sections, via the two named
3607	% obligations: lem:base_change_map_affine_local (locality reduction on the affine
3608	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-
3609	% level value of the base-change map is cancelBaseChange). Both proved affine tilde
3610	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --

3615	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
3616	  global sections}.

3618	  \medskip
3619	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
3620	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
3621	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
3622	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
3623	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
3624	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
3625	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
3626	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
3627	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
3628	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
3629	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
3630	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
3631	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
3632	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
3633	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
3634	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
3635	
3636	  \medskip
3637	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
3638	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
3639	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
3640	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
3641	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
3642	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
3643	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
3644	  \[
3645	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
3646	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
3647	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
3648	  \]
3649	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
3650	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
3651	  of modules.
3652	
3653	  \medskip
3654	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
3655	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
3656	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
3657	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
3658	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
3659	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
3660	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
3661	  \[
3662	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
3663	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
3664	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
3665	  \]
3666	  of \(R'\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). This is the entire
3667	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
3668	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
3669	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
3670	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
3671	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
3672	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
3673	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
3674	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).

3677	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
3678	
3679	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
3680	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
3681	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
3682	
3683	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3684	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3685	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3686	  \mathlibok
3687	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3688	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3689	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3690	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3691	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3692	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3693	  \[
3694	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3695	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3696	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3697	  \]
3698	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3699	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3700	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3701	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3702	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3703	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3704	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3705	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3706	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3707	\end{lemma}
3708	
3709	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf condition as an equalizer of products]
3710	  \label{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}
3711	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isSheaf_iff_isSheafEqualizerProducts}
3712	  \mathlibok
3713	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3714	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a presheaf with values in a category with products on a
3715	  topological space, and let \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of
3716	  opens with union \(U = \bigcup_i U_i\). Then \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies the sheaf
3717	  condition on \(\mathcal{U}\) if and only if the fork
3718	  \[
3719	    \mathcal{F}(U)
3720	    \;\xrightarrow{\;\mathrm{res}\;}\;
3721	    \prod_i \mathcal{F}(U_i)
3722	    \;\underset{\mathrm{rightRes}}{\overset{\mathrm{leftRes}}{\rightrightarrows}}\;
3723	    \prod_{(i,j)} \mathcal{F}(U_i \cap U_j)
3724	  \]
3725	  is a limit (an equalizer), where the two parallel maps restrict the \(U_i\)- and
3726	  \(U_j\)-components to \(U_i \cap U_j\). This is Mathlib's
3727	  \texttt{isSheaf\_iff\_isSheafEqualizerProducts} together with the fork
3728	  \texttt{SheafConditionEqualizerProducts.fork}; it is exactly Čech degree
3729	  \(0 \to 1\) and requires no separatedness.
3730	\end{lemma}
3731	
3732	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3733	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3734	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3735	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3736	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3737	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3738	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3739	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3740	  \begin{proof}
3741	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3742	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3743	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3744	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3745	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3746	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3747	  \end{proof}
3748	\end{lemma}
3749	
3750	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3751	[$\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})$ as a finite sheaf-condition equalizer]
3752	  \label{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}
3753	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.gammaIsLimitSheafConditionFork}
3754	  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl is strictly more general than this prose — it takes ANY
3755	  % open family `U : ι → X.Opens` over ANY scheme (no QCQS / finite / affine hypotheses), giving
3756	  % `IsLimit (fork M.presheaf U)`. The finite affine cover of a QCQS scheme is the intended
3757	  % application; the consolidated existential `Modules.exists_finite_affineCover_isLimit_sheafConditionFork`
3758	  % now has its own block, Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer_cover}.
3759	  \uses{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3760	  Let \(X\) be a quasi-compact, quasi-separated scheme and \(\mathcal{F}\) a sheaf of
3761	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Fix a finite affine cover \(\mathcal{U} = \{U_i\}_{1 \le i \le t}\)
3762	  of \(X\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}. Then the module of global
3763	  sections is the equalizer of the two restriction maps of the cover,
3764	  \[
3765	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
3766	    \;=\;
3767	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3768	      \textstyle\prod_{i} \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
3769	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
3770	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
3771	    \Bigr),
3772	    \qquad
3773	    s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}}), \quad s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}}),
3774	  \]
3775	  and the products are \emph{finite} (indexed by the finite sets \(\{i\}\) and
3776	  \(\{(i,j)\}\)).
3777	  \begin{proof}
3778	    \uses{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib, lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3779	    Apply the equalizer-products form of the sheaf condition
3780	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:sheaf_equalizer_products_mathlib}) to the underlying sheaf of
3781	    \(\mathcal{F}\) and the cover \(\mathcal{U}\): since \(\bigcup_i U_i = X\), the
3782	    fork displayed there computes \(\mathcal{F}(X) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) as the
3783	    equalizer of \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j}
3784	    \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\). The cover is finite by
3785	    Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}, so both products are finite. The
3786	    module structure is inherited objectwise: the forgetful functor to abelian
3787	    groups preserves and reflects the limit, so the equalizer is computed in
3788	    \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}\) over the ground ring.

4011	  % $$
4012	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
4013	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.

4024	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
4025	  % \end{enumerate}"
4026	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
4027	  \(i = 0\) case.}
4028	  Consider the cartesian square
4029	  \[
4030	    \begin{array}{ccc}
4031	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
4032	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
4033	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
4034	    \end{array}
4035	  \]
4036	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
4037	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
4038	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
4039	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
4040	  \[
4041	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
4042	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
4043	  \]
4044	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
4045	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
4046	  \[
4047	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
4048	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
4049	  \]
4050	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
4051	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
4052	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
4053	
4054	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
4055	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
4056	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
4057	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
4058	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
4059	  an isomorphism.
4060	\end{theorem}

4120	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
4121	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech

4129	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib,

4131	        lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris}
4132	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
4133	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
4134	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
4135	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
4136	  sequence. The proof is the composite of the decomposition chain.
4137	
4138	  \medskip
4139	  \emph{Reduction to the global-sections comparison.} By
4140	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}, the base-change map of
4141	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
4142	  after reducing to affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
4143	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the module comparison map
4144	  \[
4145	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
4146	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
4147	  \]
4148	  is an isomorphism (this uses that \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent — \(f\) is
4149	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated — so each side is the tilde of its module of
4150	  global sections, and that pullback along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules).
4151	
4152	  \medskip
4153	  \emph{The comparison map is an isomorphism.} Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and
4154	  quasi-separated, \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over \(A\), so
4155	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris} applies and gives
4156	  \[
4157	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
4158	  \]
4159	  Internally that lemma presents \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as the finite
4160	  sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover
4161	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}), base changes the equalizer diagram term by
4162	  term via the affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
4163	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}), and commutes \(- \otimes_A B\)
4164	  past that finite equalizer by flatness
4165	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}), reducing the general
4166	  quasi-separated case to the separated case
4167	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}) by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the
4168	  number of cover members. Combined with the reduction above, the sheaf-level
4169	  base-change map is an isomorphism.
4170	\end{proof}

4172	\subsection{The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{eqLocus}$}{eqLocus} globalization (FBC-B)}
4173	
4174	The separated-case argument is repackaged here at the level of \(A\)-modules, where
4175	\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf, so that flat
4176	base change becomes the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
4177	(Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) applied to an honest
4178	\(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\). The blocks below construct that presentation concretely: every
4179	module of sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\) is viewed as an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along the
4180	structure-sheaf restriction \(A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), the restriction maps of \(M\) become
4181	\(A\)-linear, and the two product legs of the sheaf condition assemble into a pair of \(A\)-linear maps
4182	whose equalizer locus is \(\Gamma(X, M)\). Throughout, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is a sheaf of
4183	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) is an open cover with
4184	\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).
4185	
4186	The bijectivity of the keystone presentation rests on two element-level sheaf axioms of the underlying
4187	abelian-group sheaf \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), recorded as Mathlib dependency anchors.
4188	
4189	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf separatedness: local equality forces global equality]
4190	  \label{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
4191	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'}
4192	  \mathlibok
4193	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4194	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on a topological space \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a
4195	  family covering an open \(V\) (i.e.\ \(V \le \bigvee_i U_i\)), and \(s, t \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) two
4196	  sections. If the restrictions of \(s\) and \(t\) to every \(U_i\) agree, then \(s = t\). This is the
4197	  separatedness half of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
4198	\end{lemma}
4199	
4200	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf gluing: a compatible family has a unique amalgamation]
4201	  \label{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4202	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'}
4203	  \mathlibok
4204	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4205	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a family covering an open
4206	  \(V\), and \((s_i)_i\) a family of sections \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) that is \emph{compatible}
4207	  (the restrictions of \(s_i\) and \(s_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree for all \(i, j\)). Then there is a
4208	  unique \(s \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(s_i\). This is the gluing half
4209	  of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
4210	\end{lemma}

4212	\begin{definition}\leanok
4213	[The ground ring of global sections]
4214	  \label{def:fbcb_groundRing}
4215	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.groundRing}
4216	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{ground ring} is the commutative ring
4217	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = \mathcal{O}_X(\top)\) of global sections of the structure sheaf,
4218	  taken from the \(\mathbf{CommRing}\)-valued structure presheaf so its ring and algebra structures
4219	  resolve canonically.
4220	\end{definition}
4221	
4222	\begin{definition}\leanok
4223	[The structure-sheaf restriction ring map \texorpdfstring{$A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)$}{A to sections over U}]
4224	  \label{def:fbcb_rhoU}
4225	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU}
4226	  \uses{def:fbcb_groundRing}
4227	  For an open \(U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction along \(U \subseteq \top\) is a ring
4228	  homomorphism \(\rho_U : A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the ring map
4229	  along which sections over \(U\) are regarded as \(A\)-modules.
4230	\end{definition}
4231	
4232	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4233	[Transitivity of the restriction ring maps]
4234	  \label{lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp}
4235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU_comp}
4236	  \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
4237	  For opens \(V \le U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction
4238	  \(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\) composed with \(\rho_U\) equals \(\rho_V\):
4239	  \[
4240	    \bigl(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\bigr) \circ \rho_U = \rho_V .
4241	  \]
4242	  \begin{proof}
4243	    \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
4244	    Both sides are the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\)
4245	    along \(V \subseteq \top\), factored as \(V \subseteq U \subseteq \top\); functoriality of the
4246	    structure presheaf collapses the two-step restriction to the one-step restriction.
4247	  \end{proof}
4248	\end{lemma}

4250	\begin{definition}\leanok
4251	[Sections over \texorpdfstring{$U$}{U} as an \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module]
4252	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaModA}
4253	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaModA}
4254	  \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}

4256	  \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along \(\rho_U\) (Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_rhoU}). This is the
4257	  common \(A\)-module home in which the global-sections equalizer is formed.

4260	\begin{definition}\leanok

4262	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom}

4267	  It is built from \(M.\mathrm{map}\) by restriction of scalars, using the transitivity
4268	  \(\rho\)-coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp} to identify the two scalar actions.

4271	\begin{definition}\leanok
4272	[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction map]
4273	  \label{def:fbcb_gammaResA}
4274	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA}

4281	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4282	[The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction is the underlying presheaf restriction]
4283	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
4284	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA_apply}
4285	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA}

4287	  as the presheaf restriction of \(M\): for \(x \in \Gamma(U,M)\) and \(V \le U\),
4288	  \(\mathrm{gammaResA}(x) = (M.\mathrm{map})\,x\).

4292	    Definition~\ref{def:fbcb_gammaResAHom} leave the underlying function unchanged, so the map agrees

4297	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4298	[Functoriality of the \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-linear restriction maps]
4299	  \label{lem:fbcb_gammaResA_comp}
4300	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaResA_comp}
4301	  \uses{def:fbcb_gammaResA, lem:fbcb_gammaResA_apply}
4302	  For opens \(W \le V \le U\) and \(x \in \Gamma(U,M)\), the \(A\)-linear restrictions compose as the
4303	  single restriction:
4304	  \[
4305	    \mathrm{gammaResA}_{V \le U}\!\bigl(x\bigr) \text{ then } \mathrm{gammaResA}_{W \le V}
4306	      \;=\; \mathrm{gammaResA}_{W \le U}\!\bigl(x\bigr).
4307	  \]
4308	  \begin{proof}

4311	    restriction of \(M\); functoriality of the presheaf \(M\) collapses the composite of the two
4312	    restrictions \(W \subseteq V \subseteq U\) to the single restriction \(W \subseteq U\).
